speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Rose Minsky. Leslie Choper is the interviewer. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- about my sister-- she went from-- back from work with a whole group of people. She was holding her ID card. She was thinking, everybody was thinking that whoever got the red ID card, that works by the Germans, will be spared of the evacuation. They'd let us be in Warsaw. But it was not so. They blockaded the street, and they start catching people-- the police and the Germans, the SS, and big trucks to take them to the Umschlagplatz. The Umschlagplatz-- this was the point, the-- the place from where the trains left to the death camps. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she knew by then-- we all knew already where they taking-- taking the people, that they're taken to their deaths. This was in 19-- July 1942. So she start running. She want to run in a building, in the building. And to get to somewhere to a basement or stairs to an attic, to be spared of being sent away. And there was  an Ukrainian SS man placed on top of the roof of a five-story building, shooting down to people running away from machine gun. And he got her with a bullet, through and through. Though the back and the heart. Fell dead. That's what we were told. We were looking-- she didn't came back home. We are looking for her all over. Our cousin told us she went with her-- she managed to escape. That she fell dead. And my-- they took some of us to a morgue in Warsaw. My father-- from the whole family, one person was permitted, got permission to bury the dead. So my father was the one. I didn't even-- couldn't go. The rest of the family couldn't go. My mother couldn't go. He went to the cemetery and buried her. And they were digging a very big grave, because that time could be-- were-- in the thousands, killed people in the Warsaw ghetto. So they were going to put everybody in one common grave. But my father still had a couple hundred zlotys in his pocket. And he bribed the gravedigger, and he-- he made a grave-- he should dig a grave separate for my sister. That's what he did. Till now, I don't know where the grave is. No monument on it, nothing. I don't know where she lays. I know that she's some place on Gensia Street in the Warsaw cemetery, in the Jewish cemetery. This I know. Next day, mine-- my brother went out from the house also for some Jews. And I watched from the street-- from the window, I saw another blockade. And I said, who knows, to my mother, if my brother will come-- if he will come back. And he didn't come back. He was caught. And I don't even know where he is, where his ashes are. He was only 15 years. My sister was 18. He was 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took him that day? Did anybody see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: A day after-- after the sister. Can you imagine my mother's feeling? Our feeling? Losing one member of the family after the other-- after another one? In 1941, the first victim was my youngest brother, who died practically from starvation from lack of medicine in the ghetto. Then was my sister. A day after was-- this was in July 1942-- was my brother. From all the three children, my parents remained only with me. And then they said that everybody must work day or night, night shift and day shift. So my-- my-- my father went to the small ghetto, they took him to work. I was in the big ghetto. We were the only two members of the family left, and we were separated. We couldn't see each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was taken from that place where my father was working together with my mother. They came in at-- to the-- to the place where she was working by a machine. And she was working the military uniforms for them. And they said that women are no more needed. Women are not fast enough for the machine. And they took away all of them, and from that all. And my mother-- my father smuggled his way through somehow to come to me. He manage because he was a very good, qualified tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he made for one of the big shots, they trusted him to make a coat for his wife, and she liked very much the coat. So she said, I want to do something. Well, what can I do for you, for appreciation? So he said, the only thing you can do for me is to reunite me back with my-- with my daughter. I have lost the whole family. This is the only member that is still alive, and I would like to be with her together. So he got the permission officially, which was unbelievable. No-- at that time, to go from the small ghetto to the big ghetto, that was like to smuggle a border, from one country to the other. And he got the permission, and he came to-- to this big ghetto to took me away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I want to mention-- I think I should mention this-- that I was previously married, and I had a husband and a little child of nine months who perished in the big ghetto. I came home, and I didn't find them home no more. So I was left all alone. And I wanted very badly to be with my father. As long as I have to live, I want to live together, be with my father. So he took me back to the small ghetto. That's why I avoided-- I avoided the-- the uprising. Because the uprising took place in the big ghetto, and I was in the small. We went up on the roof, and we saw the-- the flames. We heard the detonations of the bombs. And we were very, very unhappy that we cannot take part in it. My father was only 48 years old, still felt strong enough to fight. But we had no connection. We had no connection to reunite with the people in the big ghetto, the fighters of the-- of the uprising there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after a couple months being with my father together, they decided to make Warsaw Judenrein. You know about this, Judenrein? Judenrein-- that means free of Jews. Final solution. So the rest of the Jews, they ordered us all away from the machines to go down in street. And they formed-- like in five people, we had to march to the Umschlagplatz, to that point from where the trains go away. We came to that Umschlagplatz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Ukrainians, SS, they were in charge over there-- a lot of them. First thing that they did was, we were begging for a little bit of water. And they said, if you-- [RUSSIAN], in Russian. [RUSSIAN] means give me your watch, you get water. Give me your ring, you get water. That's how they cleaned us out of our possessions. And after sitting a night in the dirt over there-- it was a horrible thing to be over there. Children, women, sick, old people-- they sat in their own dirt. They took us to the trains. They pushed us in that inhumanly way. Even cattle they treat better. They-- [NON-ENGLISH], fast, fast, fast-- with that butt of the gun hitting us. They pushed us in that train, more than the train could hold, than the wagon could hold. The wagon could hold maybe about 40 people. They pushed maybe about 200 people. We had only standing positions, we could be there. Like, we couldn't sit down. And then they separate after half the way, they seal the wagons. There was no way to escape from there. There was not such a smart person that could get out of there. Young and old and everybody there. And we knew that we were going to our deaths. We were positive that we won't survive this. There was no water. Again, the-- the-- the SS, the Ukrainians were sitting on the rooftops, with their machine guns, opening up a little-- a little window, talking down to us-- to us. We were all dying of-- for a drink of water. It was terrible hot, filthy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And ask us, [RUSSIAN]? You want-- you want water? [RUSSIAN]. So again, the same story, everybody took whatever he had for a drink of water.

speaker: interviewee
text: Half the way, they stop the train. I have train-- because they-- Majdanek, in Majdanek where they took us, they took me there, was overfilled. They found out that Majdanek is overfilled, so half of the train, the people who went to that camp, to another camp, forgot the name of it. I went to Majdanek with my father. In Majdanek later I found out that my-- they took-- they separated me from my father. He went with the men. I was with the women. I later find out from a man who was in that same transport that my father perished. They took him to the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was left alone. No living soul in this world, with no relative. All alone. And of course Majdanek we--- we had to-- we had to bring in everything. Leave behind our clothing, go through a shower. We still didn't know if the shower will be gas or will come out water from the shower.

speaker: interviewee
text: And somehow we got cold water, with some soap. And I later found out what kind of soap this was. This was not regular soap. This was piece of cake of soap with three letters-- RJF was on each piece of soap. And this stands for Rein Juden Fetts. That means clear Jewish fat. The soap was made of Jewish fat.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that people didn't know what kind of shower they were going into. Was there any rebellion, was there any reaction, even when you were getting onto the trains back in the ghetto? Or as you went to the showers?

speaker: interviewee
text: There couldn't be any reaction. We were surrounded with the gendarmes. Whoever raised their hand, whoever did something was shot right away on the spot. There was most of the people were old, sick, and children, because the young people were already taken away, or they perished in the Warsaw ghetto uprising. So what could we do?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did people hear and know what was really going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: How'd they hear? Simple. I was working in the shop. This was before I reunited with my father. Came in a man, all in rags, all scratched up and beaten, dirty. And he said to us, people, don't go willingly. Because it was a time that people went willingly. The-- the hunger was so great, so big, they didn't give us nothing to eat, the rations were so meager, that people thought that somewhere else going to get better. And they offered on the way, to the evacuees, they offered a loaf of bread and a jar of jam. And for this reason a lot of people went, because they were starved.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this man came in. He said, people, don't go. Don't let yourself get fooled. You're not going to any labor camp. You're going to die.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was working, this man, in Treblinka, in Treblinka, in that camp. And he managed somehow to escape, to hide himself between the clothes of the dead people, and they shipped the clothes to Germany. And he got in on that train, with the clothes. Somehow he managed. And he escaped. He came back to that place.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the ghetto. And he warned the people, but no-- people at this time, they fooled themselves. There was a mother, an intelligent woman, who said to him, better you leave that place and don't spread any panic. My child is there, and I go there to reunite with my child. Because she had lost a child previously. And they didn't believe him. It was hard to believe what he described. He described such a horror that people thought that he's fantasizing, that he's exaggerating, that he's off his mind. It was hard to believe. His name was Mr. Reznick. Matter of fact, that he built in Israel-- I was there in one of the kibbutzim, and there is a miniature Treblinka. He built this to show that the crematorium, where the shower came from, with those things. He built it. He's not alive no more, that man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. Reznick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Reznick. No, no. Mr.-- Mr. Wiernik. Mr. Wiernik-- not Reznick, my mistake. So he warn us. But there was nobody left to fight them. No one. The fighters, the real fighters fought. They fought for more than four weeks, and they killed some Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had their satisfaction, but they all perished. They were jumping. They didn't want to fall in-- a life to their hands. They are jumping from burning buildings. The ghetto was burned. They-- they decided to burn house by house. They sent-- they threw down bombs from airplanes-- airplanes, incendiary bombs. And then, the people-- there is only a few survivors from that fight, that I know. That one is Vladka Meed, that she smuggled in ammunition from the-- from the Polish side to the ghetto. And the-- there was a few I know from that fight. I was very unhappy that I couldn't be that time in that fight, because my father took me to the small ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go back for just one minute. You mentioned before that you were married and you had a child. Please tell me their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Their names-- he was, my husband's name was Schmuel Blu. The child's name was Nina. She was only nine months old when she perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know their fate? Where they were taken to?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were taken to die, to the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when they were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: At work. In the shop, doing my work. Coming home and not finding them at home. It was that time-- I got, in my article, this write-up, this was a time when you came home from slave work, forced work, and you didn't find your relatives. One day a sister was missing, the other day a brother was missing, one day a-- a-- grandparents were missing, a child was missing. This was those-- those days. And you had to live like this, and struggle for your survival. Every day was a struggle for survival. The hunger was so terrible there. And the epidemics in the ghetto-- typhoid epidemic, tuberculosis, all the kind-- all the kind of terrible sicknesses. And they blamed us. They took us to [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] meant disinfection, that we're dirty Jews-- we keep ourselves dirty, in those conditions that people that they were chased out from villages, from other towns. Everyone to Warsaw, to make one big, you know, evacuation point from us to be easier for them to get all the Jews from Poland. So they chased them all first into the Warsaw ghetto. And from-- and the ghetto was not prepared to have so many people, so the-- the sickness was terrible. Terrible sicknesses broke out. Hunger and sicknesses and the winter frost, suffering from-- from the cold. And they still blamed us that we created this condition-- we made the war. We created the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there access to any medical care in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. Not at all. If would be access to medical care, my youngest brother would survive. He was not-- in the beginning, he had only a sickness of enlarged glands, which, with good food and vitamins, and some maybe antibiotics, the sicknesses is being taken care of. And he died from this. Temperature got higher and higher, till he got meningitis. It went to his-- to-- to his brain. And we had to watch him two weeks, the way he was dying in the house, and we couldn't help him. We couldn't help him. I, who almost brought him up, because my mother couldn't take so much care no more, so I helped a lot. After school I didn't play with children, I was a little mother-- wheeling the carriage, diapering the baby, feeding him. I had to watch him die for two weeks. I almost died with him, and my mother, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for food, for survival in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a different story. Then my brothers-- they-- the one, they were very slim, skinny, from malnutrition. They had to slip-- they slip through, children, fed the families. Children slipped through opening in the-- in the wall and brought from the Aryan side a potato, some bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes they were shot by getting back. Getting out or getting back. Sometimes they managed, the ghetto people managed to bribe whatever they could. The-- the-- the gendarme, by the wall, who was watching-- they bribe him with money. So he make believe that he don't see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they use money to buy the things outside of the ghetto? How did they acquire it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't buy outside the ghetto. The Polish people made a whole market, a whole business out of it. They bribe the Germans. They should let them bring in food to the ghetto, some food, for fantastic prices. They made a lot of money at this time. Whoever could still afford to buy, bought. Like a piece of bread for 100 zlotys, like a piece of butter for 100 zlotys. There were not many people that could afford these prices. Who didn't have-- we died from malnutrition. In our-- in our backyard, where we lived, the-- the parents of three small children were caught in the street and sent away to Treblinka. In the house remained three little kids with no-- no care. Three little children. We, the tenants, whatever we didn't have enough for us brought down something for these kids, too, that they should be able to survive. The children got left like this, yet we shared whatever we had, that little bit with them. Till they died, too. It was a time that people died, children and older people, like flies. You could talk to a person this minute, and a few minutes later you could see him laying in street covered up with a newspaper-- with newspapers, dead. And waiting for the street-- people that they picked up, the street people, the people-- the dead people from the street to be buried. All my neighbors died like this. My family died, from my family-- aunts, cousins died. Died this way. I'm the only survivor. I have no-- beside my immediate family, I have no family, like I would be born out of a stone. And I miss them. I  still miss them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course. Did you go into hiding at any time in the ghetto during the roundups?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't go. I couldn't go to hiding. Yeah, we were-- we were hiding. Sometimes, when we heard the Germans yelling that we have to come down in the backyard, I hide a few times. But I heard the marching, the boots, and my heart was pounding. And it's still up to now. I have to live on pills, on those pressure pills. And this is from this time. Then I didn't tell you still this story about Auschwitz, about my running away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, we're going to get to that. Just want to get a complete picture of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto, to live through the ghetto. To live a few days in the ghetto was a big, terrible thing. Not everybody could live in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because-- because the conditions of living over there, they were inhuman. They were inhuman. Not created for human beings to live. You had to settle to live in dirt, and with hunger. You couldn't go-- I couldn't go on the Aryan side to hide, because I was a typical Jewish-looking girl-- black eyes and black hair. And olive skin makes you-- right away, they know you're Jewish. And if the Germans would not, the Poles would point on you that this is a Jew in hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I survived the ghetto because I was working at a place that was for them very important. The soldiers were on the Stalingrad, in Russia, fighting the German soldiers. They needed-- there were a lot of cold there, a lot of frost, a lot of snow. They needed those furs, and I was working on this. The-- the ship-- ship coats for the soldiers, that kept them warm. A matter of fact, I was two times taken away from the machine. I was an operator. And then they decided that women are not needed no more, women are not productive enough. I was taken away from the machine. And they want to send me away. I looked around, and I stayed with old women, little kids, which I knew right away where I am going. But the manager of that division went over to that SS man, pointed at me-- this was two times-- that I am very fast. Without me he can close up. There won't be any work done. I am faster as-- as the men. And they put me back to work. Two times this happened. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I managed even in Birkenau, in Auschwitz to escape a couple times to hide myself in the lavatory, to hide myself from selections. Matter of fact, they took us once from the barrack where we were working, in Birkenau, to the sauna to give blood. They had the nerve to take Jewish blood for their wounded soldiers. And I said, I would rather die, and I won't give them my blood. And I jumped out onto the SS man's back, from a high, you know, from a window. It was a ground, but was high. I jumped, and I started running to the lavatory-- and I avoided this. And they took blood. Not just a little bit, they took a lot of blood. They didn't care if the person going to die next day. The people became right away bluish, a different color. They had the nerve, they hated us, to take our blood to help them out when they in trouble, when their solders were wounded.
